text:
If you’re serious about cracking any IT job interview, mastering SQL is non-negotiable.

And here’s why I recommend it:

↳ 50 days structured learning roadmap
↳ 50 practical videos focused on real-world use cases
↳ FAANG-level questions included
↳ Designed for real interview preparation and not just theory
↳ Already trusted by 10,000+ learners

I’ve personally gone through this program, and honestly - this is one of the most practical and interview-aligned SQL learning resources available. 🙌

📍Remember: Strong SQL skills often show your database skills and is a strong factor in whether you move forward in technical interviews. It’s the foundation across Data Engineering, Data Analytics, and a requirement for many tech roles.

Try out this free & structured resource to master sql.✌️

✅Eight years in corporate. Went from a small company to FAANG. Here are 10 things I wish somebody told me in my first year 🙌

I will share this the way I’ve actually experienced it.

1️⃣. 𝐘𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐦𝐚𝐧𝐚𝐠𝐞𝐫 𝐢𝐬 𝐫𝐞𝐚𝐥𝐥𝐲, 𝐫𝐞𝐚𝐥𝐥𝐲 𝐢𝐦𝐩𝐨𝐫𝐭𝐚𝐧𝐭.
 Your growth depends a lot on who you report to. A good manager pushes you, protects you, speaks for you in rooms you’re not in. A bad one can slow you down.

2️⃣. 𝐁𝐞𝐟𝐨𝐫𝐞 𝐣𝐨𝐢𝐧𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐚𝐧𝐲 𝐭𝐞𝐚𝐦, 𝐭𝐚𝐥𝐤 𝐭𝐨 𝐩𝐞𝐨𝐩𝐥𝐞.
 Please don’t just look at the brand name or compensation. Reach out to people on LinkedIn. Ask how the team actually functions. Ask about the manager.

3️⃣. 𝐈 𝐢𝐠𝐧𝐨𝐫𝐞𝐝 𝐭𝐡𝐢𝐬 𝐨𝐧𝐜𝐞. 𝐈𝐭 𝐜𝐨𝐬𝐭 𝐦𝐞.
 I joined a team even after getting warned that it wasn’t great. Every single day I was thinking about how to get out instead of how to grow & it was a really difficult time.

4️⃣. 𝐍𝐞𝐯𝐞𝐫 𝐠𝐨𝐬𝐬𝐢𝐩.
 You genuinely don’t know who talks to whom. I’ve seen casual conversations travel across teams and levels. Be friendly. Laugh. Have fun. But don’t say things you wouldn’t want repeated.

5️⃣. 𝐈𝐭’𝐬 𝐚 𝐣𝐨𝐛. 𝐍𝐨𝐭 𝐲𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐰𝐡𝐨𝐥𝐞 𝐥𝐢𝐟𝐞.
 I used to work all the time. Late nights. Constantly asking for more work. Trying to prove myself every day.

Build your career, yes. But don’t give it everything.

6️⃣. 𝐓𝐚𝐥𝐤 𝐚𝐛𝐨𝐮𝐭 𝐲𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐰𝐨𝐫𝐤.
 If you are doing amazing work but nobody knows, it doesn’t matter. Keep your manager and TL in the loop. Do small presentations. Send updates.
You have to make it easy for someone to vouch for you.

7️⃣. 𝐀𝐥𝐰𝐚𝐲𝐬 𝐝𝐨𝐜𝐮𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐞𝐯𝐞𝐫𝐲𝐭𝐡𝐢𝐧𝐠.
 Praise from someone? Screenshot it.
 Fixed a bug? Document it.
 Spot bonus? Note it.

Keep a running yearly document of your work and achievements. When review season comes, you should have details, not faded memories.

8️⃣. 𝐃𝐨𝐧’𝐭 𝐝𝐨 𝐢𝐧𝐯𝐢𝐬𝐢𝐛𝐥𝐞 𝐰𝐨𝐫𝐤.
 Even small tasks deserve proper tracking. Create bugs. Add comments. Sometimes things look small but take hours of effort. If it’s not recorded, it disappears.

9️⃣. 𝐁𝐞 𝐟𝐫𝐢𝐞𝐧𝐝𝐥𝐲. 𝐁𝐮𝐭 𝐝𝐨𝐧’𝐭 𝐨𝐯𝐞𝐫𝐬𝐡𝐚𝐫𝐞.
 Build good relationships. Respect people. Have a good time with your team.

But maintain boundaries. Always.

🔟. 𝐀𝐥𝐰𝐚𝐲𝐬 𝐮𝐩𝐬𝐤𝐢𝐥𝐥. 𝐊𝐞𝐞𝐩 𝐥𝐞𝐚𝐫𝐧𝐢𝐧𝐠.
 You never know when you might have to interview again. Markets change. Teams change. Companies change. Stay prepared. Keep your fundamentals strong. Don’t stop learning just because you’re comfortable.

If you’re early in your career, I hope this saves you a little time.

The AI Anxiety in Software engineers is real. Here is how to handle it. 👩‍💻👇

The anxiety is real and natural with all the advancements. It’s in every DM, team meetings & coffee chats. 💬

✅With the recent release of Claude Opus 4.6, that feeling became stronger. We aren't just looking at incremental updates anymore; we’re looking at:

↳ 1M Token Context Windows: The ability to 'read' and understand an entire repository at once.

↳ Complex Logic: Executing multi-step workflows without losing the context.

↳ Market Reaction: Noticed recent tech giants stocks dip?

👉Does this mean the 'End of the Engineer'? No. But it is the end of the 'Code Monkey.'

If a model can refactor, debug, and implement structured tasks, the 'value' of an engineer has officially moved upstream.

To stay relevant, you need to master two sides of the same coin:

📍The 'New' Fundamentals - The AI Stack
You don't need a PhD, but you must understand the mechanics:

↳ Embeddings and RAG: How data becomes vectors and how models retrieve them.

↳ Inference: The reality of running these models in production.

↳ Prompt Engineering: Moving from 'chatting' to structured instruction.

📍The 'Classic' Moat - Human Strategy 
AI assists with implementation. Humans still own the architecture.

↳ System Design and Scalability: Designing for 10M users, not just 10.

↳ Trade-offs: Navigating constraints that don't have a 'right' answer.

↳ Security and Data Modeling: Ensuring the foundation is actually sound.

The edge doesn’t come from competing with the tool, it comes from being the person who knows how to wield it.💯

Stop worrying about the model replacing you, Start worrying about the engineer who knows how to use the model better than you do. 🙌

Three months in GA, and we're already transforming how teams like Brex and Drata build data workflows.

We are the first analytics platform where AI builds your data model for you.

Today, Cube agent already works in 200+ companies and has processed 500,000+ lines of semantic layer code.

Every company wants AI-powered analytics. But there's a problem no one has solved:
AI without a semantic layer hallucinates and gives you confident, wrong answers.
The only fix is a strong data model that gives AI the context to understand your business.

But building one by hand takes weeks.
So the industry is stuck: build manually and wait, or let AI guess.
We created a better option.

Cube's AI writes the semantic layer itself.
The foundation that makes every query, dashboard, and answer accurate.
No trade-off between speed and accuracy. Cube gives you both.

Here's how it works:
1. Connect your data sources. Cube scans your schema and builds a semantic model in seconds.
2. Need a new metric? Describe it — AI writes the code on the fly.
3. Generate reports and dashboards with no hallucinations.

Another uncomfortable thought:
If work is your entire personality, then you’re not living - you’re being utilised.
Some people don’t experience time.

They consume it in slots.
Even rest is 'well-deserved'.
Even joy needs justification.

They don’t sit with thoughts.
They schedule them.

The tragedy isn’t working hard.
The tragedy is outsourcing meaning to a payslip.

Philosophers warned us about this long ago -
when identity collapses into function, humans become tools that applaud their own usefulness.

Titles feel permanent until they aren’t.
Calendars feel important until they stop filling themselves.

And when work finally goes quiet - layoffs, burnout, weekends, retirement -
there’s a strange silence no KPI prepares you for.

Because if you only know how to be productive,
you don’t know how to be present.

Work should sharpen you.
Not replace you.

A life that cannot slow down
is not ambitious -
it’s afraid of stillness.

Corporate Facts That Hurt More Than Breakups 💔😂
Fact 1:
People don’t leave companies.
Companies leave people unread.

Fact 2:
'Let’s have a quick call' has destroyed more peace than any heartbreak ever has.

Fact 3:
Your performance review depends more on your manager’s mood than your actual performance.

Fact 4:
You don’t need an alarm clock.
You have anxiety.

Fact 5:
90% of 'urgent' tasks magically stop being urgent after 6 pm.
And the saddest fact?
You can go from 'We are a family' to 'Your access has been removed' in 0.2 seconds.

If you relate, come sit.
We cry on weekdays and revive on weekends.

When one airline collapses… the others show their true colours.
And unfortunately, it’s not humanity. It’s pricing strategy.

With IndiGo flights getting cancelled across sectors, thousands of passengers are stranded, panicking, and scrambling for alternatives.
And what did the other airlines do?

Quietly increase fares.
Not by ₹500… but by ₹5,000–₹10,000+ in some routes.

Look at Delhi → Mumbai today:

The same flights that were ₹5k–₹6k last week are suddenly ₹24k–₹25k+.
No festival.
No surge in fuel cost.
Just people in trouble - and a perfect opportunity to profit.

I know business is business.
But sometimes, basic humanity > revenue targets.

Imagine families trying to get home.
Students.
People travelling for medical emergencies.
Or someone who simply cannot afford a last-minute 2x fare overnight.

In moments like these, industries show what they’re truly made of.

A little empathy won’t kill the quarterly numbers.
But it might restore faith in the system.

We need pricing regulations during crises.
We need accountability.
We need humanity.

Because when airlines turn a crisis into a revenue opportunity…
it’s not 'dynamic pricing'.
It’s just disappointing.

For years, I chased every milestone.
 💼 Bigger title
 💰 Higher salary
 📈 Better company
 🏙️ Fancy city

But every time I achieved one, I felt... nothing.

Just a few days of excitement, followed by the same emptiness again.
Then one day, I asked myself -
'What if I stopped chasing and started living?'
So I did.

I stopped trying to 'impress.'
Started learning slowly.
Started eating better.
Started walking without earphones.
Started saying 'no.'
Started choosing peace over people.
And the irony?

The moment I stopped chasing success - it started finding me.
 Better work.
 Calmer mind.
 Real people.
 Healthier body.

Because when you stop running after what you think you want, life finally gives you what you actually need.

If you’ve been feeling exhausted lately - maybe it’s not burnout.
Maybe your soul just wants a break from pretending.

Job hunting in India is not for the weak.

You’ll see a post that says 'We’re urgently hiring!'
You apply in 30 seconds.
And they ghost you faster than your ex.

'Easy Apply' feels like Tinder.
You swipe, they ignore.
And somewhere, HR writes - 'We’ll get back to you.'

You update your resume 47 times, change fonts, remove hobbies, add buzzwords like 'team player', 'growth mindset', and 'Excel wizard'…
Still no reply.
Meanwhile on LinkedIn:
People with 2 connections and 0 experience write -
'I’m thrilled to announce I’ve joined Google.'
And you’re sitting there thinking -
Maybe I should be 'thrilled' to announce that I’m still unemployed.

But here’s the truth:
You’re not lazy.
You’re not behind.
You’re just living in a country where 'experience required: 3 years' comes in intern listings.
So hang in there.

Because one day, you’ll get that offer.
And your 'We’re happy to have you onboard' email will feel like a national award.

Just Launched: AI Diet Planner Web App!

Excited to share my latest project — an AI-powered web application that generates personalized Indian diet plans using Google Gemini AI.

This project wasn’t easy. I faced real-world challenges like:

• Import & dependency conflicts
• Deployment crashes on Render
• Environment variable mistakes
• SDK migration issues
• Debugging production errors

But solving these step by step was the real learning experience.

⸻

🥗 What This App Does:

• Generates personalized AI-powered Indian diet plans
• Calculates BMI & gives goal-based suggestions
• Provides structured meals (Breakfast, Lunch, Snacks, Dinner)
• Shows estimated daily calorie intake
• Allows PDF download of the diet plan
• Includes a daily AI usage limit system (20 plans/day)
• Fully deployed backend with live hosting
• ⁠Toogle Dark Mode 🌙 

⸻

🛠 Tech Stack:

Python | Flask | Google Gemini API | HTML | CSS | JavaScript | Render | GitHub Pages

⸻

🌐 Live Project

My friend from college moved to Bangalore for her startup job.

First month was cute: 'PG life is fun!'

Month two: 'The WiFi dies every evening. Can't even do my daily standup properly.'

Month three: 'Cockroaches in the kitchen. Nobody knows who's cleaning. I just order food now.'

Month four: She quit the startup. 'Too much stress from home. Can't think straight.'

I remembered seeing Stanza Living ads everywhere. Told her, 'Those managed places have stable WiFi, housekeeping, all that. You don't deal with any of it.'

Her response: 'Too expensive for a startup salary.'

But after she quit, she calculated the cost of eating out, lost productivity, and stress eating.

Visited a property. The demo room looked good, the kitchens were clean, and there were staff members in uniform, which was professional and impressive.

She moved in on a short-term lease.

Three months later, she's back in a new startup role. 'I have evenings to chill now. I cook sometimes. WiFi works. And I’ve made a bunch of new friends.'

The old PG cost her a lot more than she thought.
Stanza Living gave her back her mental bandwidth.

Not everyone needs a managed space.
But when home stops being the problem, everything else gets easier to solve.

Everyone Laughed When I Started Posting on LinkedIn.. 💔 

Two years ago, when I started writing on LinkedIn, I used to ask my friends to drop a comment on my posts.
Most of them laughed. 'Dekho, influencer aagyi!'
It stung. More than I care to admit.
Maybe I was just embarrassing myself.
Maybe I should just stop.

And for almost 8-9 months, I did.
I stopped posting.
I lost the courage to show up.
I convinced myself that my voice didn’t matter.

But something inside me wouldn’t let go.
I remembered why I started: not for the likes, not for the claps, but to share, to help, to connect with people who felt just as lost as I once did.

So I came back. Quietly, at first.
One post. Then another.
No expectations, just honesty.

And today—against every moment I almost gave up—I made it to #66 on Favikon’s list of India’s Top LinkedIn Creators.

Not #1. Not the biggest name.
But for me? It’s everything.

Today, the same people who laughed, they scroll in silence.

I’m not here to say I’ve 'made it' or that this is the peak. There’s so much more to learn, so much more to give.
But this is for anyone who’s ever felt too small, too scared, too 'cringe' to show up.
Your day will come. ❤️ 

Thank you to everyone who believed, supported, your support means more than any rank ever could.
Here’s to the quiet ones who refuse to quit. 🫰 ♥️

Peyush Bansal started Lenskart in 2010.

16 years later, Q3 FY26 results: ₹2,308 Cr revenue, ₹132.7 Cr profit.

But here's what's interesting about his approach.

While most D2C founders chase valuation headlines, Peyush focused on fundamentals. Store economics. Supply chain. Customer retention.

Not viral. Just effective. Yeah I am writing because he has been my favourite shark since I started watching Shark Tank.

The company just posted 38% YoY revenue growth while expenses grew only 28%. That's discipline, not luck.

₹132.7 Cr quarterly profit in a capital-intensive retail business? That's years of operational groundwork paying off.

Peyush never claimed to be a genius. He claimed to be patient.

Turns out, patience with execution beats impatience with scale.

Lenskart isn't the loudest startup story. It's becoming one of the most profitable ones.

Silent execution > Loud announcements.

Trust is the hardest thing to scale, especially in a fragmented, credibility-deficit category like pre-owned cars.

For years, buying a used car in India meant doubt at every step.
Is the pricing fair?
Is the history clean?
Is the car actually as good as it looks?

Over the last decade, Spinny didn’t just participate in this market, it redesigned it.

What stands out to me isn’t just growth.
It’s the master campaign behind the brand, a decade-long commitment to becoming the most trusted name in the category.

Fixed pricing.
200-point inspections.
Standardized refurbishments.
5-day money-back guarantee.
Up to 3-year warranty.

Individually, these are features.
Collectively, they are category-defining signals.

Spinny understood early that in a trust-deficit market, brand-building isn’t about louder marketing, it’s about tighter systems. Consistency over virality. Process over promise.

10 years later, they aren’t just a platform.
They’re the benchmark.
A master in the category.
And arguably, India’s most trusted used car marketplace.

Because when you remove uncertainty, you don’t just sell cars, you scale confidence

Internship Season is Here, Make Sure Your Resume Stands Out!'
💡 A quick story: Last year, a junior at my college reached out to me. He had applied to 30+ internships but hadn’t heard back from a single one.

When I reviewed his resume, it hit me, he wasn’t doing anything wrong, but he also wasn’t doing enough right.

Here’s what I told him to fix:
1️⃣ Your Resume Isn’t a Diary
→ Most students list everything they’ve ever done. Instead, focus on relevant experiences that align with the internship role.

2️⃣ Show, Don’t Tell
→ Replace vague phrases like 'worked on projects' with specifics:

'Built a chatbot using Python that reduced customer response time by 40%.'

3️⃣ Leverage Academic Work
→ No work experience? No problem. Highlight coursework or projects that demonstrate your skills (e.g., 'Developed an e-commerce website as part of a web development course').

4️⃣ Tailor for Each Role
→ One-size-fits-all resumes don’t work. Use keywords from the job description to align your resume with the role.

5️⃣ Keep it Clean
→ ATS systems hate fancy formatting. Stick to simple fonts and layouts.

💡 The Result?
After making these changes, he landed two interviews in under two weeks, and secured an internship at a top startup!

If you’re gearing up for internship applications, make sure your resume isn’t holding you back. 

I’ve put together a guide to help you craft an internship-ready

Interview 1-2: Rejected in the first rounds.
Interview 3 (Salesforce): Cleared until interview stage
Interview 4 (Goldman Sachs): Resume mismatch.
Interview 5-8: Struggled to communicate.
Interview 9-10 (Flipkart): Finally cracked it 🎉
Interview 11 (Google): Got the internship, but no PPO.
Interview 12 (Nurture farm): Offer revoked.
Interview 13: Rejected in final rounds at top PBCs.
Final (Oracle, Confluent): Cracked it 🙌

I cleared exams of 20+ companies during placements and 5+ during internships, but rejections kept coming at the final stage.

There were moments when I honestly thought, 'Maybe this isn’t for me.'

But every rejection taught me something new. I improved my core subjects and finally got guidance from seniors. That’s how I cracked Oracle.

My biggest mistake was: I asked for help too late, after facing tons of rejections.

Thankfully, now you don’t need to guess your way alone. You can get complete upskilling + interview prep guidance from Ajay Yadava, an ex-Google engineer💪

In his cohort (limited to 20 people), you can get complete help:
→ For getting the interview: resume optimization tips, referral help, project guidance.
→ And for cracking it: mock interviews, upskilling sessions on DSA & System Design.

All you have to do is book a FREE call here: https://lnkd.in/gyWYkYBp 

You’re not lacking talent. You just need the right guidance; use it and keep going!

Your first tech job will crush your college expectations - prepare for these shocks!

Here are the reality bombs:

Shock 1: Code Quality Matters More Than Speed
- College: 'Make it work somehow'
- Industry: 'Make it maintainable, scalable, testable'

Shock 2: You'll Debug More Than You Code
- 60% time understanding existing systems
- 20% fixing bugs
- 20% writing new features

Shock 3: Documentation Is Not Optional
- Your clever code means nothing if teammates can't understand it
- Comments save careers, not just time

Shock 4: Meetings Are Actually Important
- Understanding requirements prevents weeks of wrong development
- Technical discussions shape better solutions

Shock 5: Continuous Learning Is Mandatory
- Technologies evolve faster than college curriculum
- Self-learning becomes your superpower

My Reality:
First month: Confused by codebase complexity
Third month: Contributing meaningful features
Sixth month: Optimizing critical systems

What's your biggest fear about your first tech job?

This popped up on my feed today. Multiple times.

I’m genuinely amazed at how far brands are willing to go today in the name of attention and recall.
The lines between clever and suggestive are getting thinner in public advertising.

This billboard clearly does its job of grabbing eyeballs - No doubts there!
But it also left me with more questions than answers about where we’re headed with mainstream marketing in shared public spaces.

Maybe this is just the new normal of the attention economy.
Or maybe we’re slowly normalising a kind of 'anything goes as long as it trends' mindset.

I’m curious to know: as marketers and consumers, how do you look at such campaigns in public spaces?

We often talk about productivity as if everyone has the same energy and body.

They don’t.

For some people, long typing sessions mean wrist pain.
For others, focusing on a blank screen is the actual hard part.

Voice-first tools quietly change that equation.

Wispr Flow already lets people on Mac, Windows, and iPhone offload a lot of typing. 100+ languages, automatic punctuation, filler removal, the works.

Android support is important not just because of market share, but because it moves that accessibility layer to more devices people actually own and use daily.

If switching from typing to talking lets someone get through their workload without burning out their hands (or their focus), that’s not a 'nice feature'. It’s the difference between barely keeping up and actually doing their best work.

The Android waitlist is live now.
If you know someone for whom typing is a tax, not just a task, this might be the link worth forwarding today:

80% of Jobs Are NEVER Posted Online (HRs Confirmed This!) 🚨
I just had coffee with an HR director at a FAANG company who shared something shocking: 'Most candidates spend 90% of their time where only 20% of jobs actually exist.'

The truth? While you're refreshing job boards hoping for interviews, hiring managers are filling positions through internal networks before posting publicly.

This explains why so many qualified candidates are getting ghosted despite sending 100+ applications.

The Hidden Job Market Is REAL
Last month, my mentee Raj was desperate after 200+ applications led to zero calls. Then he changed his approach completely:

✅ Instead of applying to job postings, he built relationships with hiring managers
✅ Instead of mass-sending resumes, he created targeted outreach
✅ Instead of competing with 300+ applicants, he got recommended before positions were announced

Result? 3 interview calls in just 2 weeks.

How to Access These Hidden Jobs:
1️⃣ Direct HR Outreach → Most hiring managers have 'potential hire' lists they check BEFORE posting publicly

2️⃣ Industry Slack/Discord Communities → Join channels where managers often share upcoming openings informally

3️⃣ Strategic LinkedIn Engagement → Comment thoughtfully on posts by hiring managers at target companies (they notice!)

4️⃣ Relationship Building > Resume Blasting → A 10-minute conversation with the right person beats 50 perfect applications

The Math Is Simple:
Traditional approach: Competing with 300+ candidates for public listings = <1% response rate

Hidden job approach: Accessing opportunities before they're public = dramatically higher success rate

Remember - in today's market, who you know often matters more than what you know. The job search game has changed in 2025. Time to change your strategy too.

2 PM. Production bug. Empty stomach.

Every developer knows this feeling.

You're deep in debugging mode.
Stack traces everywhere.
Coffee's gone cold.
And you realize you skipped lunch… again.

Last month, our team had back-to-back sprint reviews.
By 3 PM, someone joked: 'Should we order food or fix the bug first?'

We laughed. But it wasn't funny.

When your brain runs on caffeine instead of calories, even simple fixes take twice as long.

Recently discovered that some teams/companies now have pre-planned office meals, from platforms like Cookr

No decisions. No delays. Just consistent, home-style food.

Honestly, that's one less context switch during crunch time.

Comment 'meal' if you want to know how we sorted this for our team.

I’ve been building something I personally needed.

A clipboard sync tool that doesn’t rely on cloud routing.

Most clipboard solutions depend on external servers or ecosystem lock-ins.

Syncara is designed differently:

• Direct device-to-device communication
• Works over your local network (LAN)
• No external relay servers
• No clipboard storage
• Identity-based trusted pairing
• Real-time text & image transfer

Your devices talk directly to each other.

Currently supports:
 • Windows ↔ Windows (multi-device sync)
 • Android ↔ Windows
 • Android → Multiple Windows at once
 • Text and images
 • Works automatically on the same network

Linux and macOS are next.

This edition goes beyond a competition. It offers free masterclasses, mentoring by industry leaders, investor interactions, and a grand finale on campus with VCs and angel investors, guided by an experienced advisory committee.

✨ This wasn’t a YouTube video. This robot responded to our code in real time.

I’ve seen robots in videos and simulations before.
But watching one stand in front of me, respond to commands, and actually move that felt different.

During the robotics workshop, we worked with a humanoid bot, explored how vision models detect hand gestures, and connected software logic to physical motion.

One small change in code… and the robot behaved differently.

That instant feedback was powerful.
Seeing simulations turn into real actions reminded me why robotics is such an exciting field — it’s where coding stops being abstract and starts becoming alive.

This workshop didn’t feel like a lecture.
It felt like a peek into how ideas turn into machines that interact with the real world.
Moments like these quietly shape how you think about technology.

If you have an interview in the next 24-48 hours, here’s a smart last-minute SQL revision plan.

Most people don’t fail SQL interviews because SQL is hard.
They fail because they revise randomly.

1. Revise core concepts, not syntax
 Make sure you clearly understand:

* SELECT, WHERE, GROUP BY, HAVING, ORDER BY
* INNER JOIN, LEFT JOIN, RIGHT JOIN
* Subqueries vs CTEs
* Aggregate functions (COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX)

Interviewers care more about logic than memorized syntax.

2. Practice writing joins without looking at notes
 Take 2–3 tables and practice:

* Joining multiple tables
* Handling NULLs properly
* Using GROUP BY with joins
 Many candidates lose marks here.

3. Be very clear with GROUP BY + HAVING
 Common interview trap:
 WHERE filters before grouping
 HAVING filters after grouping

If you mix this up, even easy questions feel hard.

4. Window functions = big advantage
 Revise:

* ROW_NUMBER()
* RANK() and DENSE_RANK()
* PARTITION BY

Many companies now expect basic window function knowledge.

Connect Vishakha Singhal ❤️ 
Repost it to share in your network 

5. Solve 3–5 real interview-style questions
 Focus on:

* Second highest salary
* Nth highest salary
* Finding duplicates
* Running totals
* Top N per group

These patterns repeat everywhere.

6. Practice explaining your query out loud
 In interviews, clarity > speed.
 Explain:

* Why you used JOIN instead of subquery
* Why you used window function instead of GROUP BY
* Time complexity in large datasets

7. Don’t ignore edge cases
 Think about:

* NULL values
* Empty tables
* Duplicate records
* Case sensitivity

Good interviewers test thinking, not memorization.

8. Revise indexing basics
 Know:

* What an index is
* When it improves performance
* When it can slow down writes

Even a basic explanation makes you stand out.

Final advice:
Don’t try to learn 20 new concepts in one night.
Sharpen what you already know.

Connect Vishakha Singhal ❤️ 

SQL interviews are not about writing 200-line queries.
They are about clean logic, clarity, and confidence.

Save this before your next interview.
